narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 42% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students with a higher likelihood of passing.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance, particularly your grades in Year 1 and Year 3, played a significant role. Your Year 3 decile ranking was slightly below average, which contributed to the prediction that you might struggle with the bar exam. Similarly, your Year 1 decile ranking also had a notable impact, suggesting that your early academic performance could be improved.

Your LSAT score was close to the average, which means it didn't strongly sway the prediction in either direction. However, your family income, being in the upper-middle bracket, was a positive factor, as students from this background often have access to more resources. Your undergraduate GPA was slightly below average, which added to the concern about your academic readiness.

Interestingly, your part-time status was a factor, as most students in the dataset were full-time, and full-time students generally have more time to dedicate to their studies. Your gender and racial background had minimal influence on the prediction.

To improve your chances of passing the bar exam in the future, consider focusing on enhancing your academic performance, particularly in areas where you can improve your grades. If possible, transitioning to full-time study might also provide you with more time to prepare. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive outcome but rather an opportunity to identify areas for growth. With dedication and effort, you can work towards achieving your goal of passing the bar exam.
